steps.title: From outcomes to chances
steps.body: Probability turns uncertainty into math: count outcomes, use complements, and update beliefs when conditions change. This chapter covers overlapping events, independence, conditional probability, probability trees, and counting methods.

definitions.definition: The probability an event does NOT happen: P(not A) = 1 − P(A).
P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B). Subtract the overlap so it isn't counted twice.
One event happening doesn't change the other's probability; then P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B).
P(A | B) is the probability of A given B has happened — restrict to the B group, then divide.
A branching chart of sequential outcomes; multiply along branches, add across them.
Permutations count arrangements where order matters; combinations count selections where it doesn't.

steps.title: One outcome on a fair die
steps.context: A fair six-sided die is rolled once.
steps.prompt: Tap the die face for rolling a 5.
hints: For equally likely outcomes, probability is favorable outcomes divided by total outcomes.
There is exactly one favorable face (5) out of six faces.

feedback.wrong: Count favorable outcomes over total equally likely outcomes.

solution.body: P(5) = 1/6 because exactly one die face is a 5 out of six equally likely faces.
solution.takeaway: Single-event probabilities on fair devices come from simple counting.

steps.title: Use the complement
hints: The complement rule says P(not A) = 1 - P(A).
From 3.1, P(5) = 1/6, so subtract from 1.

solution.takeaway: Complements are the fastest way to handle "not" events.

steps.title: Addition with overlap
steps.context: Draw one card from a standard 52-card deck.
steps.prompt: Tap the cell with cards that are **both** heart and face (the overlap).
hints: The overlap is the cell that is **both** a heart **and** a face card.
Heart face cards are J, Q, K of hearts → that's the cell showing **3**.

feedback.wrong: Tap the overlap cell: heart and face.

solution.body: The overlap is **3** (J, Q, K of hearts). By the addition rule, P(heart ∪ face) = (13 + 12 − 3)/52 = 22/52 = **11/26**.
solution.takeaway: Avoid double-counting by subtracting the overlap in OR problems, then write the probability over the full sample space (52).

fraction.prompt: Now use the addition rule. What is P(heart **or** face) for one draw from the 52-card deck? Enter it as a fraction.
hints: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B). Add hearts (13) and face cards (12), then subtract the overlap (3) you just tapped.
13 + 12 − 3 = 22 favorable cards out of 52 → 22/52 (which reduces to 11/26).

feedback.wrong: Add 13 + 12, subtract the overlap 3, all over 52: (13 + 12 − 3)/52.

steps.title: Two independent flips
steps.prompt: Flip two fair coins, then choose P(HH).
hints: For independent events, multiply probabilities.
P(H on first) = 1/2 and P(H on second) = 1/2.

steps.title: Bag draw simulator
steps.prompt: A bag has 5 red and 3 blue marbles. Draw ten pairs of marbles and ask: what is P(both red)? Start **without replacement**, run batches, then compare empirical rates to the theoretical value.

feedback.ready: Without replacement the two draws are **dependent**, so use the multiplication rule with a *conditional* second factor: P(both red) = P(1st red) × P(2nd red | 1st red) = (5/8) × (4/7) = 20/56 = **5/14 ≈ 35.7%**. Drawing a red first leaves only 4 reds among 7 marbles — that shrinking denominator (8 → 7) is exactly what makes the second draw depend on the first. Now switch to **with replacement**: the marble goes back, the draws become *independent*, and the second factor stays 5/8, giving P = (5/8)² = 25/64 ≈ 39.1%. It's higher because the first red is never used up, so the bag stays "red-rich" for the second draw. In both cases, the more pairs you draw the closer your empirical hit rate creeps to the theoretical value — that's the law of large numbers in action.

steps.title: Without replacement
steps.context: A bag has 5 red and 3 blue marbles. Draw 2 without replacement.
hints: Without replacement, the second probability changes after the first draw.
Compute (5/8)(4/7) and simplify.

feedback.wrong: Use changing denominators for no-replacement draws.

solution.takeaway: No replacement creates dependence across draws.

steps.title: Conditional probability from a table
steps.context:
Student survey:
Plays sports & has part-time job = 24
Does not play sports & has part-time job = 12
Plays sports & no job = 12
Does not play sports & no job = 52
steps.prompt: Given a student has a part-time job, tap the cell with students who play sports.
hints: "Given a job" means restrict to the **Has job** column — ignore the no-job students entirely.
Within the Has-job column, tap the *plays sports* row (the **24**).

feedback.wrong: Condition first: stay inside the Has-job column, then find plays-sports.

solution.body: Restrict to the **Has job** column: 24 + 12 = 36 students. Of those, 24 play sports, so P(plays sports | has job) = 24/36 = **2/3**.
solution.takeaway: Conditioning changes the sample space to the given subgroup, then you divide within it.

fraction.prompt: Now compute the conditional probability. Out of everyone with a part-time job, what is P(plays sports)? Enter it as a fraction.
hints: Conditioning shrinks the sample space to just the Has-job column: 24 + 12 = **36** students.
Of those 36, 24 play sports → 24/36, which reduces to **2/3**.

feedback.wrong: Use only the Has-job column: (plays sports) ÷ (all job-holders) = 24/36.

steps.title: Total probability on a tree
steps.context: Machine A makes 60% of parts with 2% defective. Machine B makes 40% with 5% defective.
steps.prompt: Use the tree. What is the overall probability a random part is defective?
hints: Add defect paths from each branch: P(A and D) + P(B and D).

feedback.wrong: Weight each defect rate by that machine's production share.

solution.takeaway: Total probability is a weighted average across branches.

steps.title: Permutations vs combinations
steps.context: Ten students run for three distinct offices: president, vice president, secretary.
hints: If roles are different, order matters.
You need 10P3 = 10 x 9 x 8.

feedback.wrong: Not quite. Ask yourself: if two candidates swapped which office they hold, would that be a different outcome or the same one?

solution.takeaway: Combinations ignore order; permutations assign roles.

steps.title: At least one defective
steps.context: Each circuit board is defective with probability 0.035. A sample has 20 independent boards.
hints: Use the complement: P(at least one) = 1 - P(none).

solution.takeaway: For "at least one," the complement is usually the cleanest path.

steps.title: Chapter 3 complete
steps.body:
You used complements, overlap, conditional probability, trees, and counting methods to solve core probability problems.

Next up: binomial distributions and how repeated yes/no trials create full probability models.

review.title: Complement rule
hints: The complement rule: P(not A) = 1 − P(A).

solution.takeaway: An event and its complement sum to 1.

review.title: Two heads
review.prompt: What is P(both heads), as a decimal?
hints: The flips are independent — multiply.

solution.takeaway: For independent events, multiply the probabilities.

review.title: Independent events
review.context: A and B are independent with **P(A) = 0.5** and **P(B) = 0.2**.
hints: Independent → multiply.

feedback.wrong: Multiply the two probabilities for independent events.

solution.takeaway: Independence means the joint probability is the product.
